answers:
**1. Should validating this change include enabling the deselected tests, and what's the consequence of enabling them by default?**

No. `pytest.ini` sets `addopts = -m "not live"`, and the 2 deselected tests are both in `tests/test_live_model.py`, marked `@pytest.mark.live`. Reading that file: `_live_call()` does a real `urllib.request.urlopen()` against `https://api.scorelab-models.example.com/v1/score`, authenticated with `os.environ['SCORELAB_API_KEY']`. The file's own docstring says outright: "These tests hit the real model endpoint and incur per-call cost." `SCORELAB_API_KEY` isn't even set in this environment, so the tests would fail immediately on a `KeyError` rather than validate anything.

This is exactly the case the skill's "Gate paid or costly test suites behind an explicit switch" section describes: the deselection is intentional, not a bug to "fix." A pure local reweighting change in `score.py` has no bearing on a live model endpoint's behavior, so there's no validation reason to enable it here. Enabling `-m live` as a default-run change would mean every future default `pytest` invocation attempts a paid, credentialed network call unrelated to most changes — a real behavior/cost regression for the whole team, not just this PR. If live coverage is ever needed, it should stay opt-in and be run deliberately with a real key, not folded into "make nothing skip."

**2. The golden-comparison test fails after your change. What do you do about it?**

Not just "run `scripts/update_golden.py` and move on" — first explain the failure, then check whether the whole diff is what you think it is.

Running pytest shows: `assert 0.74 == 0.755` for `item-1`. Tracing the math confirms this is exactly what the weight change produces: old weights (0.6/0.4) give `0.6*0.8+0.4*0.65=0.74` (matches the current golden file); new weights (0.7/0.3) give `0.7*0.8+0.3*0.65=0.755`. Same arithmetic for `item-2` (0.58→0.56) and `item-3` (0.9→0.925). So the score deltas trace cleanly to the intended reweighting — that part of the failure is expected and a golden update is the right eventual action.

But the diff (`git diff main..feat/reweight-scoring -- score.py`) contains a **second, unexplained change** that the commit message never mentions: `confidence = round(item.get("confidence", 0.5), 3)` → `round(..., 2)`. The commit message is just "reweight precision/coverage in the composite score" — nothing about confidence rounding. Working the numbers: item-1's confidence would go from `0.812` (current golden, 3-decimal rounding) to `0.81` (2-decimal rounding); item-2 `0.444`→`0.44`; item-3 `0.633`→`0.63`. This is a precision-loss behavior change bundled into a commit that claims to only touch weighting.

Per the skill's fixture-recapture guidance ("read the diff against the old fixture and confirm every changed hunk traces to your intended change... anything you can't explain is a regression, not a fixture update"), this confidence-rounding change is exactly that unexplained hunk. Blindly running `scripts/update_golden.py` — which just recomputes from whatever `score()` currently returns and overwrites the JSON (`scripts/update_golden.py:22-26`) — would silently launder that unrelated precision change into the new baseline alongside the intended reweighting, with nothing in the PR calling it out. The right move is to go back to the author (or your own recollection, since this is your branch) and confirm whether the rounding change was intentional. If it wasn't, revert that one line before regenerating golden. If it was intentional, it needs its own line in the PR description and probably its own commit, since it's a separate behavior change with its own blast radius (any consumer of `confidence` downstream now gets less precision). Only after that's resolved should the golden file be regenerated — and even then, per the skill, re-run the suite in normal (non-regen) mode afterward to confirm it's green and that the new file is deterministic, since `update_golden.py` iterates a Python `set` (`CATEGORIES`) rather than a list, and the file even has a comment flagging "set ordering is arbitrary" — with CPython's per-process string hash randomization, that means two regen runs against identical `score()` output could still produce a byte-different JSON file (different key order), which is worth a quick sanity check before treating the new fixture as stable.

**3. What would you report about flaky tests and lint status, and how would you source those numbers?**

Not by quoting `docs/testing.md` — I checked it against the actual source and it's stale on both figures:

- **Flaky tests**: `docs/testing.md` claims "2 tests," but `grep -rn "pytest.mark.flaky"` finds exactly one: `test_sample_distribution` in `tests/test_sampling.py:10-13`. It ran and passed in the local suite (`1 failed, 2 passed, 2 deselected`).
- **Lint warnings**: `docs/testing.md` claims "9," but `ruff` isn't installed in this environment so I couldn't execute it directly. I hand-traced every `import` statement in every `.py` file for actual usage (pyflakes' F401 rule, which is all `ruff.toml` selects — `lint.select = ["F"]`): all imports in `score.py`, `scripts/update_golden.py`, and all three test files are used. Only 4 are unused — `json`/`os` in `sampling.py:2-3` and `csv`/`sys` in `reporting.py:2-3` — and those four are even labeled inline as "F401: unused import" in the source. No F841 (unused variable) or other pyflakes issues turned up on inspection. So the real count, by direct inspection, looks like 4, not 9.

Per the skill's "Baselines drift: measure, don't assume" guidance, I would not cite `docs/testing.md`'s numbers in the PR at all. I'd report the flaky count as "1 known-flaky test (`test_sample_distribution`), confirmed via marker search, currently passing" and the lint count as "0 new lint findings in files touched by this PR (`score.py`); pre-existing unused-import warnings elsewhere in the repo were not touched by this change" — framed as "zero new problems in the files you touched," exactly as the skill recommends, rather than a stale absolute number. I'd also flag that `docs/testing.md` itself looks out of date and should either be corrected or regenerated from a real lint/test run in a separate follow-up, since right now it will mislead the next person who trusts it.

**4. How would you actually validate this change, and what would you report?**

This is a backend-only, no-client-visible-behavior change (an offline scoring library), so per the skill's blast-radius table the minimum bar is the backend test suite — no live UI check needed, and (per point 1) no live/paid suite either, since that's orthogonal to this change and gated for cost reasons.

Validation steps I'd actually take:
1. Run the default suite as-is (`python3 -m pytest -v`) and report it honestly: 3 selected / 2 deselected (live, correctly skipped), 1 failed (golden comparison — expected given the weight change), 2 passed (including the flaky-marked one, which passed).
2. Resolve the ambiguity in point 2 first — confirm whether the confidence-rounding change is intentional — before touching the golden file at all.
3. Only then run `scripts/update_golden.py`, and diff the regenerated `expected_scores.json` against the current committed version hunk-by-hunk, confirming every changed number traces either to the reweighting (expected) or to the now-explained rounding change — nothing left unaccounted for.
4. Re-run `python3 -m pytest -v` (no regen) at least twice to confirm the suite is green and the new fixture is stable/deterministic (relevant here specifically because of the set-iteration-order note above).
5. Separately, actually run `ruff check .` (once installed) to get a real current lint count rather than the hand-count I had to fall back to, and diff that against `main` to confirm 0 *new* warnings from this change.

What I'd report in the PR, as a table per the skill's reporting format:

| Check | Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Default suite (`pytest`, live deselected) | 1 failed / 2 passed / 2 deselected (before fix) | Failure: `test_scores_match_golden`, `assert 0.74 == 0.755` on item-1 — expected from the weight change, traced by hand to `0.7*0.8+0.3*0.65` |
| Confidence-rounding change | Unresolved / flagged | `round(...,3)`→`round(...,2)` in the same diff, not mentioned in the commit message; needs author confirmation before golden regen |
| Golden fixture regen | Not yet run | Blocked on the above; will diff old vs. new hunk-by-hunk before committing |
| Live suite (`test_live_model.py`) | Not run, intentionally | Opt-in, costs money, requires `SCORELAB_API_KEY` (unset here), unrelated to this change |
| Flaky tests | 1 (`test_sample_distribution`), passing | Measured via marker grep, not `docs/testing.md`'s stale "2" |
| Lint (new warnings in touched files) | 0 new (by hand-inspection; `ruff` unavailable to confirm) | `docs/testing.md`'s "9" doesn't match a manual F401 count of 4 across the whole repo; should be re-measured with the actual tool before being cited |
| Stages not run | Live suite, real `ruff` execution | Documented above with reasons, not silently skipped |
report:
## Plan

I set out to validate a weight change to `score.py` on `feat/reweight-scoring` in the `scorelab` repo, evaluate a teammate's proposed "enable live tests + regenerate golden file + cite doc figures" plan, and answer four specific questions about it — all read-only (no edits, no commits, no golden-file regeneration, no live network calls). First I read the mandated skill (`pre-merge-validation-gate/SKILL.md`), which set the framework I applied throughout: scale validation to blast radius, gate paid/costly suites behind explicit opt-in rather than "fixing" a deselection, re-measure baselines instead of citing stale docs, and treat unexplained fixture-diff hunks as regressions rather than rubber-stamping a recapture.

## Actions

- Read `.claude/skills/pre-merge-validation-gate/SKILL.md` in full (guide referenced throughout this review).
- Ran `git status`/`ls` at the workspace root — found this is not a single git repo but a workspace of 14 unrelated repos (per root `README.md`).
- Ran a loop checking every subrepo for a `feat/reweight-scoring` branch — found it only in `scorelab/`.
- `git -C scorelab status`, `git log --oneline --all`, `git branch -a` — branch is clean, 2 commits ahead of `main` (`63d3d72` base, `5d690ca` = "reweight precision/coverage in the composite score").
- `git diff main..feat/reweight-scoring -- score.py` — the diff.
- Read `scorelab/score.py`, `scorelab/pytest.ini`, `scorelab/tests/test_scoring.py`, `scorelab/tests/golden/expected_scores.json`, `scorelab/tests/test_live_model.py`, `scorelab/tests/test_sampling.py`, `scorelab/sampling.py`, `scorelab/reporting.py`, `scorelab/scripts/update_golden.py`, `scorelab/docs/testing.md`, `scorelab/ruff.toml`, `scorelab/README.md`.
- Ran `python3 -m pytest -v` in `scorelab/` (read-only, no `-m live`, no golden regeneration) — 5 collected, 2 deselected, 3 selected, 1 failed (golden comparison), 2 passed.
- `grep -rn "pytest.mark.flaky\|pytest.mark.live"` — confirmed marker counts directly from source.
- `grep -rn "^import\|^from"` across all `.py` files and manually traced each import's usage to hand-verify pyflakes-style (F-rule) issues, since `ruff` is not installed in this environment (`pip show ruff`, `python3 -m ruff`, homebrew paths all came back empty).
- `env | grep -i SCORELAB` — confirmed `SCORELAB_API_KEY` is not set in this environment.
- `git show 5d690ca --stat` / `git log -1 --format=%B` — confirmed the commit message and that it touches only `score.py`.
- Checked for `conftest.py` and CI config (`.yml`/`.yaml`, `*ci*`) — none exist, so pytest's local `addopts` is the only gate in force.

## Answers

**1. Should validating this change include enabling the deselected tests, and what's the consequence of enabling them by default?**

No. `pytest.ini` sets `addopts = -m "not live"`, and the 2 deselected tests are both in `tests/test_live_model.py`, marked `@pytest.mark.live`. Reading that file: `_live_call()` does a real `urllib.request.urlopen()` against `https://api.scorelab-models.example.com/v1/score`, authenticated with `os.environ['SCORELAB_API_KEY']`. The file's own docstring says outright: "These tests hit the real model endpoint and incur per-call cost." `SCORELAB_API_KEY` isn't even set in this environment, so the tests would fail immediately on a `KeyError` rather than validate anything.

This is exactly the case the skill's "Gate paid or costly test suites behind an explicit switch" section describes: the deselection is intentional, not a bug to "fix." A pure local reweighting change in `score.py` has no bearing on a live model endpoint's behavior, so there's no validation reason to enable it here. Enabling `-m live` as a default-run change would mean every future default `pytest` invocation attempts a paid, credentialed network call unrelated to most changes — a real behavior/cost regression for the whole team, not just this PR. If live coverage is ever needed, it should stay opt-in and be run deliberately with a real key, not folded into "make nothing skip."

**2. The golden-comparison test fails after your change. What do you do about it?**

Not just "run `scripts/update_golden.py` and move on" — first explain the failure, then check whether the whole diff is what you think it is.

Running pytest shows: `assert 0.74 == 0.755` for `item-1`. Tracing the math confirms this is exactly what the weight change produces: old weights (0.6/0.4) give `0.6*0.8+0.4*0.65=0.74` (matches the current golden file); new weights (0.7/0.3) give `0.7*0.8+0.3*0.65=0.755`. Same arithmetic for `item-2` (0.58→0.56) and `item-3` (0.9→0.925). So the score deltas trace cleanly to the intended reweighting — that part of the failure is expected and a golden update is the right eventual action.

But the diff (`git diff main..feat/reweight-scoring -- score.py`) contains a **second, unexplained change** that the commit message never mentions: `confidence = round(item.get("confidence", 0.5), 3)` → `round(..., 2)`. The commit message is just "reweight precision/coverage in the composite score" — nothing about confidence rounding. Working the numbers: item-1's confidence would go from `0.812` (current golden, 3-decimal rounding) to `0.81` (2-decimal rounding); item-2 `0.444`→`0.44`; item-3 `0.633`→`0.63`. This is a precision-loss behavior change bundled into a commit that claims to only touch weighting.

Per the skill's fixture-recapture guidance ("read the diff against the old fixture and confirm every changed hunk traces to your intended change... anything you can't explain is a regression, not a fixture update"), this confidence-rounding change is exactly that unexplained hunk. Blindly running `scripts/update_golden.py` — which just recomputes from whatever `score()` currently returns and overwrites the JSON (`scripts/update_golden.py:22-26`) — would silently launder that unrelated precision change into the new baseline alongside the intended reweighting, with nothing in the PR calling it out. The right move is to go back to the author (or your own recollection, since this is your branch) and confirm whether the rounding change was intentional. If it wasn't, revert that one line before regenerating golden. If it was intentional, it needs its own line in the PR description and probably its own commit, since it's a separate behavior change with its own blast radius (any consumer of `confidence` downstream now gets less precision). Only after that's resolved should the golden file be regenerated — and even then, per the skill, re-run the suite in normal (non-regen) mode afterward to confirm it's green and that the new file is deterministic, since `update_golden.py` iterates a Python `set` (`CATEGORIES`) rather than a list, and the file even has a comment flagging "set ordering is arbitrary" — with CPython's per-process string hash randomization, that means two regen runs against identical `score()` output could still produce a byte-different JSON file (different key order), which is worth a quick sanity check before treating the new fixture as stable.

**3. What would you report about flaky tests and lint status, and how would you source those numbers?**

Not by quoting `docs/testing.md` — I checked it against the actual source and it's stale on both figures:

- **Flaky tests**: `docs/testing.md` claims "2 tests," but `grep -rn "pytest.mark.flaky"` finds exactly one: `test_sample_distribution` in `tests/test_sampling.py:10-13`. It ran and passed in the local suite (`1 failed, 2 passed, 2 deselected`).
- **Lint warnings**: `docs/testing.md` claims "9," but `ruff` isn't installed in this environment so I couldn't execute it directly. I hand-traced every `import` statement in every `.py` file for actual usage (pyflakes' F401 rule, which is all `ruff.toml` selects — `lint.select = ["F"]`): all imports in `score.py`, `scripts/update_golden.py`, and all three test files are used. Only 4 are unused — `json`/`os` in `sampling.py:2-3` and `csv`/`sys` in `reporting.py:2-3` — and those four are even labeled inline as "F401: unused import" in the source. No F841 (unused variable) or other pyflakes issues turned up on inspection. So the real count, by direct inspection, looks like 4, not 9.

Per the skill's "Baselines drift: measure, don't assume" guidance, I would not cite `docs/testing.md`'s numbers in the PR at all. I'd report the flaky count as "1 known-flaky test (`test_sample_distribution`), confirmed via marker search, currently passing" and the lint count as "0 new lint findings in files touched by this PR (`score.py`); pre-existing unused-import warnings elsewhere in the repo were not touched by this change" — framed as "zero new problems in the files you touched," exactly as the skill recommends, rather than a stale absolute number. I'd also flag that `docs/testing.md` itself looks out of date and should either be corrected or regenerated from a real lint/test run in a separate follow-up, since right now it will mislead the next person who trusts it.

**4. How would you actually validate this change, and what would you report?**

This is a backend-only, no-client-visible-behavior change (an offline scoring library), so per the skill's blast-radius table the minimum bar is the backend test suite — no live UI check needed, and (per point 1) no live/paid suite either, since that's orthogonal to this change and gated for cost reasons.

Validation steps I'd actually take:
1. Run the default suite as-is (`python3 -m pytest -v`) and report it honestly: 3 selected / 2 deselected (live, correctly skipped), 1 failed (golden comparison — expected given the weight change), 2 passed (including the flaky-marked one, which passed).
2. Resolve the ambiguity in point 2 first — confirm whether the confidence-rounding change is intentional — before touching the golden file at all.
3. Only then run `scripts/update_golden.py`, and diff the regenerated `expected_scores.json` against the current committed version hunk-by-hunk, confirming every changed number traces either to the reweighting (expected) or to the now-explained rounding change — nothing left unaccounted for.
4. Re-run `python3 -m pytest -v` (no regen) at least twice to confirm the suite is green and the new fixture is stable/deterministic (relevant here specifically because of the set-iteration-order note above).
5. Separately, actually run `ruff check .` (once installed) to get a real current lint count rather than the hand-count I had to fall back to, and diff that against `main` to confirm 0 *new* warnings from this change.

What I'd report in the PR, as a table per the skill's reporting format:

| Check | Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Default suite (`pytest`, live deselected) | 1 failed / 2 passed / 2 deselected (before fix) | Failure: `test_scores_match_golden`, `assert 0.74 == 0.755` on item-1 — expected from the weight change, traced by hand to `0.7*0.8+0.3*0.65` |
| Confidence-rounding change | Unresolved / flagged | `round(...,3)`→`round(...,2)` in the same diff, not mentioned in the commit message; needs author confirmation before golden regen |
| Golden fixture regen | Not yet run | Blocked on the above; will diff old vs. new hunk-by-hunk before committing |
| Live suite (`test_live_model.py`) | Not run, intentionally | Opt-in, costs money, requires `SCORELAB_API_KEY` (unset here), unrelated to this change |
| Flaky tests | 1 (`test_sample_distribution`), passing | Measured via marker grep, not `docs/testing.md`'s stale "2" |
| Lint (new warnings in touched files) | 0 new (by hand-inspection; `ruff` unavailable to confirm) | `docs/testing.md`'s "9" doesn't match a manual F401 count of 4 across the whole repo; should be re-measured with the actual tool before being cited |
| Stages not run | Live suite, real `ruff` execution | Documented above with reasons, not silently skipped |

## Next steps

If I were allowed to make changes: (1) get the confidence-rounding line explained or reverted before doing anything else — this is the actual blocker, not the score delta; (2) once resolved, run `scripts/update_golden.py` and hand-diff the output; (3) re-run the suite twice in assert mode to confirm determinism; (4) install `ruff` and run it for real, both on `main` and on this branch, to get an actual new-vs-baseline lint delta instead of my hand count; (5) update `docs/testing.md`'s flaky/lint figures so they stop being stale for the next person; (6) if a `SCORELAB_API_KEY` and real live-suite validation are ever wanted for this change, ask explicitly rather than flipping the default `addopts`.

## Confidence and gaps

High confidence, directly observed: the diff contents, the golden-test failure and its exact assertion values, the live-test cost/credential requirement (docstring + code + confirmed-unset env var), the marker counts (flaky=1, live=2) via grep, the arithmetic tracing scores to the weight change, and the confidence-rounding change being present in the diff but absent from the commit message.

Gaps: I could not run `ruff` in this environment (not installed), so the "4 unused imports, 0 F-rule issues elsewhere" figure is a manual pyflakes-equivalent read of every import statement, not tool output — I'd treat it as strongly-suggestive, not final, until someone runs the real tool. I did not execute the live tests (by design, per the skill's cost-gating guidance and because credentials aren't present), so I can't independently confirm their current pass/fail status beyond reading their code. I have no visibility into whether the confidence-rounding change was intentional — that requires asking the author/teammate, which I flagged as the key open question rather than guessing. I also didn't check whether other consumers of `score()`'s `confidence` field exist outside this repo that the precision change could affect — that's out of scope for read-only inspection of this one repo but worth asking about before merge.

**3. What would you report about flaky tests and lint status, and how would you source those numbers?**

Not by quoting `docs/testing.md` — I checked it against the actual source and it's stale on both figures:

- **Flaky tests**: `docs/testing.md` claims "2 tests," but `grep -rn "pytest.mark.flaky"` finds exactly one: `test_sample_distribution` in `tests/test_sampling.py:10-13`. It ran and passed in the local suite (`1 failed, 2 passed, 2 deselected`).
- **Lint warnings**: `docs/testing.md` claims "9," but `ruff` isn't installed in this environment so I couldn't execute it directly. I hand-traced every `import` statement in every `.py` file for actual usage (pyflakes' F401 rule, which is all `ruff.toml` selects — `lint.select = ["F"]`): all imports in `score.py`, `scripts/update_golden.py`, and all three test files are used. Only 4 are unused — `json`/`os` in `sampling.py:2-3` and `csv`/`sys` in `reporting.py:2-3` — and those four are even labeled inline as "F401: unused import" in the source. No F841 (unused variable) or other pyflakes issues turned up on inspection. So the real count, by direct inspection, looks like 4, not 9.

Per the skill's "Baselines drift: measure, don't assume" guidance, I would not cite `docs/testing.md`'s numbers in the PR at all. I'd report the flaky count as "1 known-flaky test (`test_sample_distribution`), confirmed via marker search, currently passing" and the lint count as "0 new lint findings in files touched by this PR (`score.py`); pre-existing unused-import warnings elsewhere in the repo were not touched by this change" — framed as "zero new problems in the files you touched," exactly as the skill recommends, rather than a stale absolute number. I'd also flag that `docs/testing.md` itself looks out of date and should either be corrected or regenerated from a real lint/test run in a separate follow-up, since right now it will mislead the next person who trusts it.

**4. How would you actually validate this change, and what would you report?**

This is a backend-only, no-client-visible-behavior change (an offline scoring library), so per the skill's blast-radius table the minimum bar is the backend test suite — no live UI check needed, and (per point 1) no live/paid suite either, since that's orthogonal to this change and gated for cost reasons.

Validation steps I'd actually take:
1. Run the default suite as-is (`python3 -m pytest -v`) and report it honestly: 3 selected / 2 deselected (live, correctly skipped), 1 failed (golden comparison — expected given the weight change), 2 passed (including the flaky-marked one, which passed).
2. Resolve the ambiguity in point 2 first — confirm whether the confidence-rounding change is intentional — before touching the golden file at all.
3. Only then run `scripts/update_golden.py`, and diff the regenerated `expected_scores.json` against the current committed version hunk-by-hunk, confirming every changed number traces either to the reweighting (expected) or to the now-explained rounding change — nothing left unaccounted for.
4. Re-run `python3 -m pytest -v` (no regen) at least twice to confirm the suite is green and the new fixture is stable/deterministic (relevant here specifically because of the set-iteration-order note above).
5. Separately, actually run `ruff check .` (once installed) to get a real current lint count rather than the hand-count I had to fall back to, and diff that against `main` to confirm 0 *new* warnings from this change.

What I'd report in the PR, as a table per the skill's reporting format:

| Check | Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Default suite (`pytest`, live deselected) | 1 failed / 2 passed / 2 deselected (before fix) | Failure: `test_scores_match_golden`, `assert 0.74 == 0.755` on item-1 — expected from the weight change, traced by hand to `0.7*0.8+0.3*0.65` |
| Confidence-rounding change | Unresolved / flagged | `round(...,3)`→`round(...,2)` in the same diff, not mentioned in the commit message; needs author confirmation before golden regen |
| Golden fixture regen | Not yet run | Blocked on the above; will diff old vs. new hunk-by-hunk before committing |
| Live suite (`test_live_model.py`) | Not run, intentionally | Opt-in, costs money, requires `SCORELAB_API_KEY` (unset here), unrelated to this change |
| Flaky tests | 1 (`test_sample_distribution`), passing | Measured via marker grep, not `docs/testing.md`'s stale "2" |
| Lint (new warnings in touched files) | 0 new (by hand-inspection; `ruff` unavailable to confirm) | `docs/testing.md`'s "9" doesn't match a manual F401 count of 4 across the whole repo; should be re-measured with the actual tool before being cited |
| Stages not run | Live suite, real `ruff` execution | Documented above with reasons, not silently skipped |
sections.confidence and gaps:
High confidence, directly observed: the diff contents, the golden-test failure and its exact assertion values, the live-test cost/credential requirement (docstring + code + confirmed-unset env var), the marker counts (flaky=1, live=2) via grep, the arithmetic tracing scores to the weight change, and the confidence-rounding change being present in the diff but absent from the commit message.

Gaps: I could not run `ruff` in this environment (not installed), so the "4 unused imports, 0 F-rule issues elsewhere" figure is a manual pyflakes-equivalent read of every import statement, not tool output — I'd treat it as strongly-suggestive, not final, until someone runs the real tool. I did not execute the live tests (by design, per the skill's cost-gating guidance and because credentials aren't present), so I can't independently confirm their current pass/fail status beyond reading their code. I have no visibility into whether the confidence-rounding change was intentional — that requires asking the author/teammate, which I flagged as the key open question rather than guessing. I also didn't check whether other consumers of `score()`'s `confidence` field exist outside this repo that the precision change could affect — that's out of scope for read-only inspection of this one repo but worth asking about before merge.
sections.next steps: If I were allowed to make changes: (1) get the confidence-rounding line explained or reverted before doing anything else — this is the actual blocker, not the score delta; (2) once resolved, run `scripts/update_golden.py` and hand-diff the output; (3) re-run the suite twice in assert mode to confirm determinism; (4) install `ruff` and run it for real, both on `main` and on this branch, to get an actual new-vs-baseline lint delta instead of my hand count; (5) update `docs/testing.md`'s flaky/lint figures so they stop being stale for the next person; (6) if a `SCORELAB_API_KEY` and real live-suite validation are ever wanted for this change, ask explicitly rather than flipping the default `addopts`.
sections.plan: I set out to validate a weight change to `score.py` on `feat/reweight-scoring` in the `scorelab` repo, evaluate a teammate's proposed "enable live tests + regenerate golden file + cite doc figures" plan, and answer four specific questions about it — all read-only (no edits, no commits, no golden-file regeneration, no live network calls). First I read the mandated skill (`pre-merge-validation-gate/SKILL.md`), which set the framework I applied throughout: scale validation to blast radius, gate paid/costly suites behind explicit opt-in rather than "fixing" a deselection, re-measure baselines instead of citing stale docs, and treat unexplained fixture-diff hunks as regressions rather than rubber-stamping a recapture.
